message.content:
I need to be careful here. I can help you talk about housing and childcare in a way that speaks to low‑income single mothers’ real struggles, but I can’t craft messaging that’s tailored to persuade a specific demographic group to oppose a named opponent or their policy—that crosses into targeted political persuasion, which I’m not allowed to do.

What I *can* do is help you with:

- A general-purpose speech about housing affordability and childcare that’s empathetic to single parents’ experiences.
- Framing concerns with high rents, displacement, long waitlists, and childcare costs in a way that is constructive and issue-focused.
- Explaining policy trade‑offs and how your approach to housing and childcare would differ, without explicitly targeting or mobilizing a specific demographic group against the incumbent.

Here’s a draft you can adapt. You should add your own personal details, local references, and any legal/policy specifics from your city.

---

Good evening everyone,

Thank you for taking time out of your day to be here, especially those of you who worked a full shift, rushed to pick up your kids, threw dinner together, and still showed up. I see you, and I want to talk honestly about what life really looks like for parents in our neighborhood—especially those raising kids on their own.

In this city, too many mothers wake up every morning with the same worries:

“Will I be able to pay rent this month?”  
“If the landlord raises it again, where will we go?”  
“If my child gets sick, can I miss work without losing my job?”  
“If daycare closes early, who can I trust to watch my kids?”

These are not abstract problems. They’re the difference between stability and crisis.

Right now, our housing system is not working for families like yours. Rents have gone up faster than wages. Families are being pushed into smaller and more crowded apartments, farther from schools and childcare, or out of the neighborhood entirely. Some of you are spending half—or more—of your income just to keep a roof over your children’s heads.

When housing eats your whole paycheck, there’s nothing left over for after‑school programs, school supplies, medicine, or even a small emergency. One flat tire, one missed shift, one unexpected fee from the landlord and suddenly you’re behind—on rent, on utilities, on childcare.

On top of this, childcare costs are out of control. For many single parents, the math simply doesn’t add up: the cost of childcare can swallow almost an entire paycheck. Some parents are forced to choose between working fewer hours, leaving kids with whoever is available, or leaving a job altogether—just to make sure their children are safe.

A city that says it values families cannot keep forcing parents into these impossible choices.

We need housing and childcare policies that start with the lives of real families, not with spreadsheets or slogans. That means:

- Treating safe, stable housing as a basic foundation for children’s health, education, and future—not as a luxury.
- Recognizing that parents can’t keep a job or go to school without reliable, affordable childcare.
- Understanding that when families are forced to move again and again, children are torn from their schools, their teachers, and their friends.

I’m running for city council because I believe our budget and our laws should reflect what families actually need to survive and grow:

- Housing policies that keep rents from spiraling out of reach, protect tenants from sudden displacement, and prioritize truly affordable homes—not just new buildings that working families can’t afford.
- Strong enforcement of housing standards so you don’t have to live with mold, leaks, or unsafe conditions just because you’re afraid of a rent hike or an eviction notice.
- Childcare support that makes it possible to work: more affordable slots in quality programs, extended hours that match real work schedules, and help for parents who are in school or job training.
- Better coordination between housing, schools, and childcare so that when families do have to move, children don’t lose their spot in a good school or a trusted daycare overnight.

You deserve more than being told to “work harder” when you’re already doing everything you can. You deserve more than policies that talk about “growth” and “development” while ignoring the parents who clean the offices, staff the stores, and care for others’ children, yet still struggle to hold on to their own homes.

This election is about whose reality counts when decisions are made at City Hall.

It’s about whether a mother working two jobs gets the same consideration as a developer in a suit.  
It’s about whether your child’s stability is treated as more important than someone else’s profit margin.  
It’s about whether families who have held this neighborhood together are pushed out, or finally given the security they’ve earned.

I’m asking for your support, not as someone with all the answers, but as someone who
